speaker: interviewer
text: This is the beginning of tape number 7 in an interview with Emanuel Mittelman. OK, when we left off, you were at your hometown. Your-- your two brothers had come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: One.

speaker: interviewer
text: One brother. And then you had found out that your sister was also alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were going to get her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I'm going to get her. So my task was now, again, to jump from truck and train and go on foot to reach a certain city where I heard that she is there-- staying there. And I made it in a few days. I don't remember exactly what it was-- two or three days-- where I met her. And she brought home from concentration camp a girlfriend who-- she designated, while she was in camp, that, if she ever comes out alive, she designated that girl to be my brother's wife. It sounds-- it sounds funny, but that's the truth. And the truth is that she later on married my brother Joe. And we came home. And we started running a household. So now, of course, we get back. There are some other people who survived and came back to town and were beginning to build the religious life again, around us. You know, you can't find a person who was a ritual slaughterer to come get some kosher food. But you can live on other items than-- than meat and still maintain a certain kind of a kosher home. And my sister, who was older than I am, tried to get me back to-- to get back in the steps that I left over when we left our hometown, to, little by little, come back into religion, go back to start putting on your phylacteries, your tefillin, daily, and do your daily prayers. It took a little while. It wasn't so easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were so out of it that somehow the freedom affected you in such a manner that all the stuff that we once saw in Auschwitz, all these horrors, somehow got you to a point to think-- when we had these-- these atheists-- if I should call them that, maybe I shouldn't call them that-- tell guys like myself, who were known there that we come from a religious circle, taking us out when we hear the cries and the-- and the-- and see the burning of the fires, say, you still believe there is a God? After all that stuff? Somehow, it had an aftereffect on me when I came home. And somehow-- I don't know whether it was subconsciously or consciously-- I began to, like, shed all the religion. I says, I-- it can't be true that there is such a thing. That-- that-- that-- you know, that God is around. And he runs the whole world watching all that misery. Is such a thing possible?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took a short while until I shook all this, what shall I say-- 'foreign thinking,' I will call it for the lack of a better word. But the-- the religion that was imbued within me and that has nothing to do, I think, maybe with my parents or with the education that I had, it was-- it was purely that-- I consider myself lucky of picking it up again and continuing. Because I can't see myself living a life without it. Yet, I know that there are many friends who were my pals in school and in the Jewish school, who threw everything away. And I've never in my life blamed them. As a matter of fact, I always tell my children that the ones who came back and are nonbelievers-- they are the normal ones. The ones of us who do believe, they are the crazy ones. Because no normal human being, unless he's made out of iron or steel-- his will-- can go through all that stuff and come back and still-- believing. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I really am appreciative to-- to whoever helped me to that end, to stay and be religious. Because I have a feeling that deep in me, within me, there was always a ray of hope. I don't care how small it was. It might have been so minuscule that you probably can't describe it. But there was in me always a minuscule belief that, one day, maybe, just maybe I'll see freedom again. And I'll be able to continue in the path I once started. Of course-- and I will be purely commentary, now, if I may add this, that the school-- this is a thought that I always also tell my children. I tried to imbue them. What Hitler did to me, of course, I don't wish it on anybody. And I don't wish it on myself, either. And I would have never wanted him to do that. But this learning-- what I learned from that and the different human being that it made out of me, is worth to me millions of dollars. Because of what I went through has taught me a lessons. First of all, never to lose your head, no matter what the situation is. I have never in my life worried if I miss a plane, if I sleep over a station, because there's no hotel or there is temporarily no food. Nothing shakes me out of [INAUDIBLE]. Because I know I close my eyes for a minute, and I look back where I was over 50 years ago, and I said, nothing can measure up to do this. And when I open my eyes, I know I have it better. So I would say that faith and belief in God is my second nature, so to say. I-- I don't pat myself on the shoulder and say to myself, it's your doing. I mean, I created that faith. Where it comes from, I don't know. Faith is not something you can describe. I just know that I believe. And I know, from the previous thing that I mentioned to you, especially with the bullets flying around me, that there was a guardian angel sent by God to catch all these bullets and not let them penetrate me. And, of course, a hundred different incidents, even the thought of taking charcoal and grind it to make your own prescription might also be a nuance of a-- of a good angel giving me the thought. Hey, do that, and maybe that will save you. You grasp, so to say, in danger at a straw. When you fall from a building and you see a-- a rope that is made out of straw, you'll try to hold onto it. Maybe that will save you. And that's the experiences that I went through. And I think that helped me carry on.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the non-Jewish population of Michalovce--

speaker: interviewee
text: Michalovce.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what-- what was their reaction when you came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no accolades. There were no welcomes. There were no-- I don't remember anybody coming over and say-- oh, I'm so glad you were saved. It-- it was at least very cool. And-- and because of that, because it was so cool, you didn't see the population pouring their hearts out. There were certain places maybe where it did happen. But by us, I have neither heard, nor experienced a warm welcome from the non-Jewish population that says, oh, I'm so thankful to God that you came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever find out what happened to the rest of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father and my mother and my older brother and younger brother and younger sister were taken away in 1943 to Poland, to some camp. I don't even know the name. It wasn't a concentration camp. It was more like a ghetto where all the people were together.

speaker: interviewee
text: And by pure incidence-- again, where I can see God's finger in that-- my oldest brother was once in Munich, in a synagogue-- excuse me-- humming a tune of religious nature to himself. And a neighbor sitting in the synagogue turns around to my brother and said, where do you know that tune from? He says, what do you mean, where do I know it? He says, my father used to sing this every Friday night in his house. That happened in a synagogue on a Friday night. So he said, what is your name? He says, my name is Mayer Mittelman. Your father's name was Ya'akov Shmuel, he says-- Jakob Samuel? He says, yes. So my brother looks in bewilderment. How do you know? So he was the life witness who told us the date when my parents and my brothers and my sister were shot by the Germans as a mass killing. He named the city, too. I don't recall the city anymore. But by this coincidence, by humming a tune, we found out the date. And we know today exactly the day when we observe for our yearly return of when we say prayers on behalf of my parents and my brother and sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, it shows you that-- that there must be something higher than us human beings running this world. It might not happen to everybody. But there are little things, little signs. And if you're able to read them, I think that nothing in this world moves without a superior being, decreeing that it should, that it ought to move.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you reach a decision to leave Slovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't too long after I was there. We tried to get in-- in some dealing and wheeling on the black market, there, while we were home, to get back on our feet, and so forth. I mean, there was no, really-- there was no income and so forth. And just to sustain ourself. I mean, a little food and so forth. And there was a Jewish community-- used to get some support from the-- from the-- some Jewish organizations through the United States. They were very helpful. We looked around ourself. We saw how few people came back. We saw what happened in the city. And we hear the-- the whole story on what's going on. We saw that there is no future, as far as-- as far as Judaism, to bring up children or something. Even if you get married one day, that this is not a place where to stay. We realized this very early, so we decided, all of us, that we're going to go eventually-- leave the place and go to Israel. I mean, that was our dream, at that time. It didn't turn out to be like this. But that's the decision we made. Of course, by that time, we found out that our oldest brother-- of whom we all thought we had an eyewitness, so to say, that he's not alive anymore-- that he's also alive someplace in Germany. So we decided that we are going to leave Czechoslovakia. I think it was in the beginning of '48. Maybe earlier yet. Haven't got an exact date. No, it was in '47. And, first of all, I made that trip that way I made the trip before, to get my sister. The person who came to tell me he's still alive in Israel, that my brother's alive, that he saw him personally, of course I wouldn't believe it. Because if you live two years and somebody told you, listen, I myself saw your brother dead, then you don't have to believe right in a second if somebody told me, I saw him alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he described me where and when. He's in Munich. And I can give you an address. I'll tell you, I still see you don't believe me. I will go back with you. And I'll show you where he is. And he did!

speaker: interviewee
text: And we made a trip. It took us a week to get-- oh, and that's an hour trip in an airplane, today. It took us-- again, because, you know, trains were derailed and so forth. And we got to Munich. And, sure enough, I found my brother alive, my oldest brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, needless to say, again, we raise our hands in thanks to the Almighty that-- that we met. Especially that's a double joy, because here-- here we thought that he's dead. And he told me that he's not-- he doesn't want to come back home.

speaker: interviewee
text: He maybe come for a visit, but that's it. There wasn't still the time. And we decide to leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he did come back for a visit. They were occupying a German-- some Nazi's home in Munich that they were given by the American authorities. So they lived there real nicely. It was a villa.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- we travelled back home-- tell my brother and my sister, hey, I saw him. I know he's alive. I mean, now, we can believe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's-- so we were planning. And, by the time, we decided we were going to leave Czechoslovakia, we're going to go to Germany and eventually make our way to Israel. So we are talking about another year, let's say-- when was Israel declared a country? I think in '48.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was just very close before that. There was a lot in the talk. The UN was dealing with the stuff and so forth. We came to Germany. We lived in Germany. I ended up in a day camp-- in a DP camp, rather. And-- and in that DP camp, I became sick. And I contacted-- I got TB.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the authorities from the UNRRA-- which was the United Nations Relief and Rehabilitation Administration-- had their doctors and stuff there. And they took me to a hospital to-- that was that camp name was Leipheim. They took me to a hospital which is Gauting, by Munchen, which is in Bavaria, where I stayed there for about a year in the sanitarium. And, of course, I was regularly visited by my sister and my brother. By that time they were already in Munich, all of them, together.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in February 1948, I was lucky. I was contacted before by a lady who, through the war, did a lot of work for Jewish people, for homeless people. Her name was Mrs. Sternbuch. She's not alive anymore. But somebody wrote a book about her. That book is called And she did immense work. She really put her life on-- on-- for hundreds of people. She worked together with an Italian priest. She was several time-- she was a Swiss woman. She was several times locked up in jail, because of her dealings and wheelings, as everything what she was doing was against the law at that time. But she came to Germany, met me in that camp. And she told me that she's going to get me to Switzerland to a sanitarium where the air is much better. And I will have a chance to recuperate there and become healthy again, which she did. And in 1948, I came to Switzerland. And in Switzerland-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] again, I was in Davos, in a sanitarium there for quite a while. And that's the place where I met my wife. She was also sick. She came from Belgium. And within a year, we were married.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got, in the Zurich Jewish community, a job as a kosher milkman. And I was, again, sitting on the top of the world. Here, I'm sitting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I got this job, I used to get 15 Swiss francs a month from some organization, Jewish organization. This, you know-- we had food. But we didn't have any money. So here I find this job. And I'm getting all of a sudden $150 American a month. So I'm being envied by all the DP guys who are also in Switzerland. And that's where we started building a new life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the rest your family? What-- what-- where did they go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, since I became sick, of course, I gave up the idea and the thought of going to Israel. And my sister, my two brothers, Joe and Mayer, they came to America while I was in the sanatorium. And my sister didn't want to go by herself to Israel, even though there was somebody who wanted to marry her and go to Israel to live there. But she refused. Because she wanted to be with her brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they all ended up here in Detroit. First, in New York, really. And eventually, my sister saw that here in America-- she was very, very religious. I mean, to a very, very high extent-- that she saw that, at that time-- life was a little different than today. She will never find a partner whom she would marry that would be religious enough for her to make a life here. So she got in correspondence to some people. And she met a guy whom she met first in Germany, whom she refused to marry. And she married him and lives in Tel Aviv now in Bnei Brak. Let me ask you this. Wasn't Mayer, your brother-- wasn't he married before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was married before the war. His wife never came back. And his child never came back. Of course, she came to Auschwitz with a child. And her chance to live was nil. And then, after the war, he remarried. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And he had two children. One of them died in 1954 when I came to America-- polio. And the other one is still alive. She's in-- she's in Israel, today.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came to America when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to America in May 1954.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have how many children, now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have six children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have 22 grandchildren. You're not asking me how many great-grandchildren-- two.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many great--

speaker: interviewee
text: Two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that your experiences during the Holocaust impact you on a daily basis?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say to the better. I mean, something good comes out of it as far-- I derive from my experiences benefits that without the experiences I would have never have gotten. I look at the world different. I look at things different. I-- as I mentioned before, nothing throws me out of my base so easily. Nothing upsets me so easily. I think I don't take things for granted anymore. That whatever is thrown in your lap is-- you know, you appreciate things more. You come to a point where you say, I never believed that I will ever see freedom. And I reach a-- a-- a stage where not only did I see freedom, but I was able to marry. I was able to have children. I was able to have grandchildren. And I watch my grandchildren get married and have great-grandchildren. It is a-- is a-- in a way, you feel you have overcome your enemy. And it gives you a-- a boost in a manner that you could be on the very bottom of hell. And if someone higher degree decrees, that it should change, it changes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Anything else you'd like to add? Anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I would like to add, for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the period of time that we live in right now, specifically viewing the situation in Israel, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that the world, the non-Jewish world, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] has very quickly forgotten. 50 years is quick. To me, it is. I mean, it's flown by like nothing as what the world once did to annihilate 6 million Jewish people with women, men, and children. They have a Holocaust center in Israel, just for a million and a half little babies that were killed. And we're now on the verge of watching how this same UN, who jumped at that time, with Russia's first vote to declare Israel as a country are already now to give everything up and to-- instead of rebuild, to destroy everything that was so hardly-- hardly-- I mean, with hard efforts-- earned. And it looks to me, with my eyes at the world, that the world forgets too quick. If you watch today Zaire, Yugoslavia, whatever-- these third-world countries-- people are starving to death. People-- and people in the world, even though America I think is the most charitable country in the world. But when you don't see it-- and some of them, even, we see it on television-- people just don't give a hoot. They keep on living. And it sounds to me-- to me, it sounds that the world-- the non-Jewish world-- I have to say there are always exceptions to the rule. And I don't like to put the whole world in one bundle. But for the purpose that I'm speaking now, I am saying that the non-Jewish world is more readily to feel bad for a killed Jew than for a live Jew. I mean, that's my personal opinion. Everybody will mourn-- oh, they killed 6 million. I remember in 1967 when the Israeli war was, where French, where Swiss people were standing on the street-- non-Jewish people-- selling oranges in every corner. I mean, just about anybody-- to make a few dollars, to be able to send it to Israel, non-Jewish people. Fine. But today, the world has changed. I mean, I'm the last person in this world who wouldn't like to see in Israel peace between the Arabs and the Jews. But a peace like we Americans have with the Canadians, where you don't have to worry to walk down the street and somebody will stab you in the back. And I'm sure that that kind of a peace, even the most ultra, ultra Jewish person in Israel will adhere to and will agree to. But history repeat itself. People don't learn anything from the history. And we know that from previous stages where anti-Semitism comes up and little by little, thank God we have, in America, here, a country where somehow in some way it's being controlled, where we have plenty incidents where we know that anti-Semitism is flourishing. I mean, we have to be on the lookout. We have to be on the watch-out. And we have to have God guarding his-- watching us with his two hands. I mean, it's all in his hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: You haven't talked about your experiences much, have you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 19-- let's see-- 1951 or '52, I had big problems emotionally with my past. Meaning, I had big problems that I never knew when to stop eating. Because in my subconscious, I thought I'm still in Auschwitz. And if I don't eat that meal, then tomorrow there won't be any. So I never knew when to stop. And I was always overeating. And I didn't feel well about it. It really hurt me, my stomach was so full.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I also had tremendous dreams. I mean, I used to get these dreams in the middle of the night. I used to wake up in the middle of the night. My wife would shake me and says, what's happening? And Nazis were-- were-- were shooting me. And people were persecuting me. And they were running after me and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: By that time, I was reading every book that I could lay my hands on about Auschwitz, about any concentration camp, watching every movie, talking about it day and night. So in my own mind, by my own way of thinking, I thought, maybe if I stop talking so much about it, maybe it'll get out of my dream. Maybe I'll change.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I started measuring, on my own, the food I eat. And I also began completely call a halt to reading papers about Auschwitz, about any camp, or reading books or watching any movies. I did that to myself. It was a self-analysis.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I saw it helps me little by little. And it went to such an extent that I have really made up my mind and I-- since that minute on, that when I came to the United States-- I was once interviewed by a Dr. Crystal. He's still the official doctor for the German consulate here in Detroit. I understand he was also in concentration camp. And he asked me, at that time-- because I was sick-- they was supposed to determine a certain pension for me. And he asked me I should tell him or relate to him a story that happened to me in Auschwitz, anything that I was just about-- that I told you about today in the movie. And I adamantly refused. And he was telling me that if I don't tell him any kind of a story, I am bound to be refused to get any kind of help from the-- monetary help from the German government. And I answered him. I remember like today. I said, my health, my well-being, is to me worth more than any money a German can ever pay me. And I refused to tell them any story. And it wasn't until [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] about maybe six or seven years ago that I was constantly nudged and being called on the telephone from different institutions to report my-- my incidents. Whatever happened to me, I should put it on film. Because it's important that the future generation should be able to hear about it, in person, from somebody who went through it. And I was asked and asked so many times. And I constantly kept refusing. Until one day, I thought I'm going to dare. And I'm going to give a trial interview, which I did, with Dr. [INAUDIBLE], and see how the results will be, whether it affects me adversely or not. And from then on, I came out all right with that. From then on, I began really speaking about it. And I speak to groups. And I used to go to the different schools and-- and tell part of my story, not as elaborate as I did right now. And thank God, it doesn't have any adverse effect on me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's pause here. This is the end of tape seven-- interview with Emanuel Mittelman.
